title: How to Open a Demat Account
meta_description: Step-by-step guide to opening a Demat account in India. Compare Zerodha, Groww, Angel One. Understand charges, documents needed, and how it works.

hero.one_liner: A Demat account holds your shares and securities electronically — like a bank account but for investments

sections.heading: What is a Demat Account?
content: A Demat (Dematerialized) account holds your stocks, bonds, ETFs, and mutual fund units in electronic form — just like a bank account holds your money. When you buy shares on the stock exchange, they're credited to your Demat account. When you sell, they're debited.

You need a Demat account to buy and sell stocks, ETFs, IPOs, and government bonds directly. For mutual funds via SIP, you technically don't need one (apps like Groww handle it), but having one gives you more flexibility. In India, CDSL and NSDL are the two depositories that maintain Demat accounts, and you access them through brokers like Zerodha, Groww, or Angel One.

sections.heading: Top Brokers Compared
sections.footnote: All three are SEBI-registered and safe. Choose based on your experience level.

steps.title: Choose a broker
steps.description: For beginners: Groww (simplest interface) or Zerodha (best platform). Download their app and click 'Open Account'.

steps.title: Enter basic details
steps.description: Name, email, phone number. You'll receive an OTP to verify your mobile.

steps.title: Complete e-KYC
steps.description: Enter PAN number, Aadhaar number, and verify via Aadhaar OTP. Upload your signature photo. Some brokers require a short selfie video.

steps.title: Link bank account
steps.description: Add your savings account details for fund transfers. Verify by uploading a cancelled cheque or bank statement.

steps.title: e-Sign and activate
steps.description: Sign the application electronically using Aadhaar OTP. Account is typically active within 24-48 hours. You'll receive your Demat account number and trading credentials.

faqs.question: Is Demat account needed for mutual funds?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Apps like Groww, Kuvera, and Paytm Money let you invest in mutual funds without a Demat account. But if you want to invest in stocks, ETFs, or IPOs, you need one.

faqs.question: Is opening a Demat account free?
faqs.answer: Yes, most brokers offer free account opening. Some charge an annual maintenance fee (AMC) — Zerodha charges ₹300/year, while Groww and Angel One charge ₹0. There's no charge for not using the account.

faqs.question: Can I have multiple Demat accounts?
faqs.answer: Yes. You can have multiple Demat accounts with different brokers. But you can only link one PAN card, so all accounts are connected to the same tax identity.

faqs.question: What happens to my shares if the broker shuts down?
faqs.answer: Your shares are safe. They're held by CDSL/NSDL (depositories), not the broker. If a broker shuts down, you can transfer your holdings to another broker. SEBI regulations protect investors.

faqs.question: Demat vs trading account — what's the difference?
faqs.answer: Demat account stores your shares. Trading account lets you buy and sell. You need both. Most brokers open both together in a single application — they call it a '2-in-1 account'.

disclaimer: This article is for educational purposes only. Stock market investments are subject to market risks. Compare broker charges on their official websites before opening an account.
